coredata.dc:description: The aim of this study was to develop konjac glucomannan (KGM)-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with different curcumin-metal chelates, where six types of films were prepared and their corresponding properties were investigated. The FTIR and XRD results showed that curcumin chelated with metal ions successfully. Moreover, curcumin-Ca chelate had the best thermal stability and antioxidant activity with the DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 38.28 % and 22.79 %, respectively. Furthermore, the results of microstructure and contact angle showed that chelation with metal ions improved the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix. Interestingly, KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with curcumin-Ca chelate (Type IV film) displayed the best thermal stability with the highest temperature of maximum weight loss at 380 °C, the best mechanical property, the highest total phenol content (17.31 mg gallic acid/g film), as well as the best antioxidant activity with DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 69.24 % and 58.66 %, respectively, and the best antibacterial activity. Consequently, Type IV film was used for the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of pork. The results showed that the pork packaged with Type IV film displayed excellent fresh-keeping properties, including reducing the increase rate of volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and pH values and the decrease rate of hardness and elasticity of pork during storage time. Meanwhile, the color of Type IV film gradually changed from yellow to red. Therefore, this study suggested that KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films have great potential application in the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of fresh meat.

head.ce:title: Development of konjac glucomannan-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with different curcumin-metal chelates: Stability, antioxidant, fresh-keeping and freshness detection properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this study was to develop konjac glucomannan (KGM)-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with different curcumin-metal chelates, where six types of films were prepared and their corresponding properties were investigated. The FTIR and XRD results showed that curcumin chelated with metal ions successfully. Moreover, curcumin-Ca chelate had the best thermal stability and antioxidant activity with the DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 38.28 % and 22.79 %, respectively. Furthermore, the results of microstructure and contact angle showed that chelation with metal ions improved the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix. Interestingly, KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with curcumin-Ca chelate (Type IV film) displayed the best thermal stability with the highest temperature of maximum weight loss at 380 °C, the best mechanical property, the highest total phenol content (17.31 mg gallic acid/g film), as well as the best antioxidant activity with DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 69.24 % and 58.66 %, respectively, and the best antibacterial activity. Consequently, Type IV film was used for the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of pork. The results showed that the pork packaged with Type IV film displayed excellent fresh-keeping properties, including reducing the increase rate of volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and pH values and the decrease rate of hardness and elasticity of pork during storage time. Meanwhile, the color of Type IV film gradually changed from yellow to red. Therefore, this study suggested that KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films have great potential application in the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of fresh meat.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of living standards, consumers are increasingly concerned about the quality and safety of food, thus it is of great importance to explore the novel food packaging with the functions of keeping food fresh and monitoring the food conditions in real time [1,2]. In our previous study, we had successfully developed novel konjac glucomannan-based highly antibacterial active films loaded with thyme essential oil (TEO) through bacterial cellulose nanofibers/Ag nanoparticles (BCNs/Ag nanoparticles) stabilized Pickering emulsions, which have good fresh-keeping effect [3]. However, it has the disadvantage of lacking real-time monitoring function. It has been demonstrated that some natural pigments (e.g., curcumin, anthocyanin) have pH sensitivity. Moreover, the pH values of the microenvironment inside the package will change as a result of some chemical substances from the packaged food during food storage [4]. For this reason, some natural pigments can be used as pH indicators in food packaging.

Curcumin (1,7-bis-(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadiene-3,5-dione) is non-toxic with pH sensitivity under alkaline conditions, antioxidant and antibacterial properties [5], which has been widely used in the field of food packaging that can monitor the freshness of the packaged food in real time [6]. However, it has some shortcomings, including poor thermal stability and low water solubility, which limit the application of curcumin as an indicator in food packaging [7]. Therefore, it is necessary to improve the properties of curcumin, and a number of strategies have been applied to stabilize curcumin and expand its relevant applications, including encapsulation into liposomes [8], the loading of nanoparticles [9], chemical modifications by polymer conjugates [10], plasmonic nanogel [11], and complexation with proteins [12]. Additionally, curcumin can form a chelate with various metal ions, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ [14] through the central β-diketone functionality of molecular structure [7,15], which is able to improve the stability and solubility of curcumin. However, there is little information about the effects of curcumin chelation on the properties of food packaging films [16].

Since then, the aim of this study was to develop the KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with different curcumin-metal chelates. Firstly, curcumin-metal chelates were synthesized, and their chemical structure, stability and antioxidant activity were investigated. Then the prepared curcumin-metal chelates were used to develop the KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films, and the microstructure, physical properties, color characteristics, transmittance of KGM-based films were studied. Meanwhile, the pH sensitivity, antioxidant and antibacterial activities of active-intelligent emulsion films were also studied. Finally, the active-intelligent emulsion films with the best properties were used for the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of pork. The parameters of the pork packaged with the prepared Type IV, including TVB-N, pH values, hardness and springiness, were investigated in the present work.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine-1oxyl radical (TEMPO), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium citrate (C6H5O7Na3), food grade glycerol, curcumin (purity 95 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), Muller Hinton agar, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and Brain Heart Infusion broth were purchased from Senbo Biology Co., Ltd. (Xi'an, China). Konjac glucomannan (KGM, food grade with the purity of 98 %) was bought from Huipu Biology Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). Thyme essential oil (TEO) was supplied by Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (China).

Staphylococcus aureus (BNCC 238481) and E. coli O157:H7 (BNCC 192101) were obtained from the College of Food Engineering and Nutritional Science of Shaanxi Normal University (Xi'an, China). Bacterial cellulose (BC) producing strain Komagataeibacter hansenii CGMCC 3917 was donated by the Fermentation Technology Innovation Laboratory of Northwest A & F University (Yanglin, Shanxi, China). Ultra-pure water was created by a Milli-Q water-purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Curcumin was mechanically mixed with CaCl2, MgCl2 and ZnCl2 (curcumin: M2+ = 1:1 mol), respectively, meanwhile the only curcumin mechanically mixed was used as control. Then, the glycerin/water (1:1, v/v) solution was added into the mixture, followed by mechanically shaking at 25 °C. The mixture was dried at 25 °C and washed with distilled water to remove free glycerin. After that, the curcumin-metal chelates were dried at 25 °C to obtain curcumin-metal chelate powders and stored in the dark, which were recoded as mechanically treated-curcumin (Cur), curcumin-Ca chelate (CurCa), curcumin-Mg chelate (CurMg) and curcumin-Zn chelate (CurZn), respectively. Moreover, the curcumin without treatment was recoded as original curcumin (O-Cur).

Bacterial cellulose nanofibers (BCNs) and bacterial cellulose nanofibers/Ag (BCNs/Ag) nanoparticles were prepared according to our previous method [3]. For the preparation of curcumin-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles, 40 mg BCNs/Ag nanoparticles were dispersed in 9 mL distilled water, and the solution was homogenized for 3 min at 12000 rpm. Then, 1 mL TEO containing 3 % (w/w, based on the weight of KGM) nothing, original curcumin, mechanically treated-curcumin, curcumin-Ca chelate, curcumin-Mg chelate and curcumin-Zn chelate were added into the solutions, respectively, and the solutions were homogenized for 3 min at 20000 rpm. Finally, curcumin-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical micrograph and particle size distribution of Pickering emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of Cur-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles was observed by digital microscope equipped with digital camera (ZEISS, Germany). The Cur-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles were dropped on the glass slide, and covered by the cover slide. Finally, the micromorphology of the Pickering emulsions was observed by digital microscope.

The particle size distribution of Cur-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles was measured by Laser particle size analyzer (LS13320, Beckman, US) according to our previous method [3]. The Pickering emulsions were diluted with deionized water, and AI and RI of samples were set as 0.010 and 1.590, respectively. Each experiment was measured three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: 0.8 % (w/v) KGM powder and 30 % (w/w, based on the weight of KGM) glycerol were dissolved in distilled water at 95 °C, followed by stirring until completely dissolved, which was recorded as the basic KGM film-forming solutions. Then, six types of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films were prepared as follows: Type I film: Pickering emulsions loaded without curcumin were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE film; Type II film: Pickering emulsions loaded with original curcumin were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE-O-Cur film; Type III film: Pickering emulsions loaded with mechanically treated-curcumin were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE-Cur film; Type IV film: Pickering emulsions loaded with curcumin-Ca chelate were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE-Cur-Ca film; Type V film: Pickering emulsions loaded with curcumin-Mg chelates were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE-Cur-Mg film; Type VI film: Pickering emulsions loaded with curcumin-Zn chelates were added into the basic KGM film-forming solutions, which was recoded as KGM-PE-Cur-Zn film. After that, the final film-forming solutions (FFSs) were homogenized at 4000 rpm for 3 min. Next, FFSs were poured into petri dishes and dried for 24 h at 40 °C. Finally, the dried films were stored in the dark.

The UV–Vis spectra of samples were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV300, InsMark, China). Samples were dissolved in 60 % ethanol solution, the measurement range was 300–500 nm, and 60 % ethanol solution was used as reference.
The FTIR spectra of samples were recorded by FTIR spectrometer (BRUKER, INVENNIO S, Karlsruhe Germany) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 [17].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded at 40 kV and 45 mA by X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Smart Lab, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were scanned from 5° to 30° with an interval angle of 0.02° [18]. The crystallinity of samples was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Crystallinity
%
=
Area of crystalline peaks / Area of
all
peaks
× 100

For scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the samples were adhered to the aluminum plate and coated with gold by sputter coater (E-1045, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan) for 60 s. SEM images were obtained by scanning electron microscope under 10 kV (S-3400N, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan) [19].

The thermostability of samples was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Thermogravimetry (TG) was conducted with 5 mg sample under nitrogen atmosphere by Thermal Analyzer System (Q1000DSC + L NCS + FACS Q600SDT, TA Instrument, New Castle, DE, USA) [20]. The samples were heated from room temperature to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, and the derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) was obtained by the derivation for the TGA curve.

The antioxidant activity of samples was evaluated by 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS) free radical scavenging assay [21]. For the DPPH assay, 1 mL sample solutions were mixed with 3 mL DPPH solution, followed by incubation for 30 min in the dark. Then the absorbance was measured at 517 nm by multiskan spectrum (Multiskan Go, Thermo Scientific, US) and the DPPH free radical scavenging activity was calculated by Eq. (2):
(2)

For the ABTS assay, 0.2 mL sample solutions were mixed with 3.9 mL ABTS solution. Then, the mixture was incubated for 10 min in the dark. Then the absorbance was measured at 751 nm by multiskan spectrum (Multiskan Go, Thermo Scientific, US) and the ABTS free radical scavenging activity was calculated by Eq. (3):
(3)

The transmittance of the films was tested by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV300, InsMark, China). The film was cut into 1 × 4 cm2 and placed into a spectrophotometer test cell. The measurement range was 300–800 nm, and the air was used as reference [22].

The mechanical properties of films, including tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB), were assessed by universal material testing machine (Minsks Co. Ltd., Xi'an, China) [23]. The film samples were cut to 2 × 8 cm2. Mechanical testing was performed with an initial grip separation of 50 mm and a cross-head speed of 50 mm/min. Each film was tested at least six times, the average value was calculated by Eqs. (5) and (6):
(5)
TS
MPa
= N /
a × b
(6)

where N is the maximum fracture force of films (N), a is the width of films (mm), b is the thickness of films (mm), L
0 is the initial length of films (mm), L is the final length of films (mm).

Contact angle values of all samples were measured by DataPhiscis OCA 20 AMP (Dataphiscis Instruments GmbH, Germany). 2 μL water drop was deposited on the air-exposed surface. The drop images were captured by high-speed video camera, and the contact angle (θ) was calculated using the ellipse equation [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thickness, water content (WC) and water solubility (WS)
ce:section.ce:para: Film thickness was measured by digital micrometer (WEIDU Co. Ltd., China) with a resolution of 0.001 mm. Measurement was taken at ten random points on each film, then the average thickness was calculated.

The water content (WC) of films was determined by measuring the weight loss of films [21]. The films were initially weighed, then dried in an oven for 24 h at 105 °C, and the final dry weight was recorded. The WC of films was calculated by Eq. (7):
(7)
WC = 100 % ×
W i
−
W d
/
W i
where W
i is the initial weight (g), W
d is the final dry weight (g).

The water solubility (WS) of the films was assessed by measuring the solubility after the films were immersed in water for 24 h. The initial dry weight of films was recorded. After immersion in distilled water at room temperature for 24 h, the wet films were dried at 105 °C for 24 h and weighed again. The WS was calculated by Eq. (8):
(8)
WS = 100 % ×
W d
−
W f
/
W d
where W
d is the initial dry weight (g), W
f is final dry weight (g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water vapor permeability (WVP) and relative oxygen transmission rate (ROT)
ce:section.ce:para:
Water vapor permeability (WVP) was measured according to our previous method [25]. A 50 mL centrifuge tube containing 30 mL of distilled water was covered with the film and secured with double-sided adhesive. The tube was placed in a desiccator containing silicon dioxide for 12 h, then the centrifugal tube was weighed. The WVP of films was calculated by Eq. (9):
(9)
WVP
g ·
m · s · Pa
− 1
=
∆ m × d
/
A × t × ∆ p
where Δm is the weight difference of centrifugal tube (g), d is the thickness of films (mm), A is the effective area of films (mm2), t is time (s), Δp is the water vapor pressure difference on both sides of films (3.168 × 103 Pa，25 °C).

Relative oxygen transmission rate (ROT) was determined according to our previous method [25]. 3 g deoxidizer (activated carbon: sodium chloride: reduced ion powder = 1.5: 1: 0.5) was placed in a centrifugal tube, then sealed with the films. The centrifugal tube was weighted, followed by putting in a desiccator containing barium chloride saturated solution at 25 °C. After 48 h, the centrifugal tube was weighted again, and the ROT value of films was calculated by Eq. (10):
(10)
ROT
g ·
m 2
· s
− 1
=
m f
−
m i
/
t × A
where m
f is the final weight of centrifugal tube after 48 h of balance, m
i is the initial weight of the centrifugal tube, t is time (s), A is the effective area of the film (mm2).

Total phenol content of films was determined by Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetrically method [26]. Firstly, 50 mg films were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water. Then 0.2 mL film extract solution, 1 mL Folin-Ciocalteu reagent and 14 mL distilled water were mixed and incubated for 8 min. Subsequently, 3 mL sodium carbonate (2 %, w/v) and distilled water were added to obtain 20 mL mixture solution. The mixture solution was stirred thoroughly and stored for 2 h in the dark, then the absorbance of the mixture solution was measured at 765 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV300, InsMark, China). The total phenol content of films was calculated by Eq. (11):
(11)
T =
C × V
/ M
where T is the total content (mg gallic acid equivalent/g dry film), C is the concentration of gallic acid procured from the calibration curve (mg/mL), V is the film extract volume (mL), M is the film samples weight (g).

Antibacterial activity of samples was evaluated by the oxford cup method [27]. S. aureus and E. coli O157: H7 were used as test bacteria. The bacterial strains were overnight cultured at 180 rpm and 37 °C. Then, the oxford cup containing sample solutions was placed on Muller Hinton agar plate, which had been previously seeded with 100 μL tested bacteria. The space between Muller Hinton agar plates and sample solutions was removed through pressing. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the diameter of inhibition zone was measured.

The films were cut into 2 × 2 cm2 and immersed in solutions with varying pH values (pH = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13), and the color response was evaluated by observing the color change of films in different pH values. The pH of the solution was adjusted with 0.1 mol/L HCl or NaOH solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for the packaging of fresh pork
ce:section.ce:para: Four groups of fresh pork were used in the present study, which were treated without packaging, packaged with cling film, packaged with pure KGM film and packaged with Type IV film (KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with curcumin-Ca chelate), respectively. All samples were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further study.

The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) of pork was measured by Kjeldahl apparatus (Dimension Icon, Bruker, USA) and expressed as mg/100 g. For the pH determination, 5 g pork was mixed with 20 mL distilled water by high-shear blender, and the pH was measured with pH meter. Hardness and springiness were assessed by texture analyzer (TA.XT.Plus, Stable Micro System, UK) with the following settings:

trigger force 5 g, probe P36R, test interval 5 s, probe speed 1.0 mm/s. The color change of films, which reflected the quality of the packaged pork, was analyzed by colorimeter, and all samples were also documented with camera [28].

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed by SPSS 22.0 software. All the data were expressed as standard ± deviation (SD), and the significance detection method was the least significant difference (LSD) (p < 0.05). The resulting images were rendered by Origin 2021 software.

As displayed in Fig. 1A, the maximum absorption wavelength of original curcumin (O-Cur) and mechanically treated-curcumin (Cur) appeared at 424 nm and 425 nm in the UV–Vis spectra, respectively, which was ascribed to the characterized peak of π → π* transition in curcumin, in agreement with the results reported in literature [29]. While the maximum absorption wavelength of curcumin-Ca chelate (CurCa), curcumin-Mg chelate (CurMg) and curcumin-Zn chelate (CurZn) shifted to 432 nm, 430 nm and 428 nm, respectively. This result was attributed to that coordination bonds were formed during the chelation process of curcumin with metal ions, which would lead to the redistribution of electron clouds and changing the energy level of electron transition inside the molecule [7]. In addition, the conjugated double bond system of curcumin would distort or expand to a certain extent after the coordination of metal ions, which influenced the conjugation effect of molecules, thus affecting the energy level difference of molecules and causing the change in maximum absorption wavelength [29].

The FTIR spectroscopy of all the curcumin samples was analyzed in Fig. 1B, where all samples showed characteristic peaks at 1631 cm−1, 1508 cm−1 and 1284 cm−1, attributing to the CO stretching vibration, CC vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively [16,30]. While for all the curcumin-metal chelates, the absorption peak of CO had shifted, indicating that the carbonyl group in the structure of curcumin was involved in chelation with metal ions [6]. Additionally, all the chelates exhibited the absorption peak at 3500 cm−1, which was due to the tensile vibration of the OH group in the phenyl ring, suggesting that the phenolic OH was excluded from replacement [31].

The crystal structure of all samples was analyzed by XRD in the present study. As shown in Fig. 1C, all samples displayed the characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 8.86° and 17.20°, suggesting that curcumin had a crystalline structure, in consistent with the result reported in literature [23,32]. However, the intensity of characteristic peaks in curcumin-metal chelates was weaker than that in original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin, which was due to that the formed coordination bond during the chelation process would destroy the original intermolecular interaction, resulting in a decrease in the stability of the crystal structure and an increase in the amorphous region of curcumin. In addition, the introduction of metal ions would lead to the changes in intramolecular and intermolecular configuration, increasing the disorder of the system, which would hinder the formation of ordered crystals, thus displaying low-crystallinity structures [33]. Meanwhile, the crystallinity of all samples was calculated according to the XRD spectra, where the crystallinity of original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin were 95.82 % and 95.13 %, respectively, while the crystallinity of curcumin-Ca chelate, curcumin-Mg chelate and curcumin-Zn chelate decreased to 51.21 %, 60.76 %, 81.23 %, respectively, showing that the crystal structure of the curcumin was significantly affected by chelation. In addition, the individual metal peaks could not be detected, which was probably due to that the metal atoms presented in the form of ligand, thus they could not form their own lattices [31]. The preparation mechanism diagram of curcumin-metal chelates was inferred and shown in Fig. 1D.

The thermal stability of materials is of great significance for practical application, thus the thermal stability including thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) were determined in the present work [29]. As displayed in Fig. 1E, original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin began to degrade after the temperature was higher than 185 °C, while curcumin-metal chelates began to degrade after the temperature was higher than 220 °C. Moreover, the residual mass of curcumin-metal chelates was more than that of original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin, showing that the thermal stability of curcumin-metal chelates was relatively better. DTG was the first order differential of TGA. As displayed in Fig. 1F, the maximum weight loss temperature of curcumin-metal chelates were higher than that of original curcumin (406 °C) and mechanically treated-curcumin (400 °C), where the maximum weight loss temperature of curcumin-Ca chelate displayed the highest value at 440 °C, followed by that of curcumin-Mg chelate (430 °C) and curcumin-Zn chelate (419 °C), in consistent with the TGA result described in Fig. 1E. The result was related to that curcumin had the chemical structure of β-diketone tautomerism, thus the structure was unstable and easy to degrade, while chelating with metal ions, the structural change of 1,3-diketone group in the center of the molecular structure of curcumin became difficult, thus the stability was improved [7,15]. Additionally, the thermal stability difference of curcumin-metal chelates was attributed to that the different ionic radii of metal ions, of which Mg2+ and Zn2+ had smaller ionic radii, leading to spatial repulsion between coordination groups and poor thermal stability [31].

As shown in Fig. 1G, all samples displayed antioxidant activity, which was attributed to that the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl group, keto-enol structure and conjugate structure that had stable electron donor property and could eliminate free radicals by forming stable products, resulting in the termination of the radical chain reactions [7,34]. The DPPH radical-scavenging activity of mechanically treated- curcumin (31.13 %) was lower than that of original curcumin (32.50 %), which was related to that the destruction of biological activity of curcumin during mechanical treatment process. However, the DPPH radical-scavenging activity of all the curcumin-metal chelates was significantly higher than that of original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin, which was attributed to that the OH bond dissociation energy (BDE) and ionization potential (IP) were decreased due to the chelation with metal ions, resulting in easier H-atom loss process in phenolic hydroxyl group and higher the ability of H-atom transfer and electron transfer reactions reactivities, thus the DPPH radical-scavenging activity was enhanced [13]. In addition, the DPPH radical-scavenging activity difference of curcumin-metal chelates was ascribed to that the electrochemical properties and coordination ability of different metal ions were different, thus the electron transfer efficiency and free radical trapping ability of the prepared different chelates were different [31,35]. Interestingly, curcumin-Ca chelate presented the best antioxidant activity (38.28 %). For the ABTS radical-scavenging activity (Fig. 1G), the trend was similar to that of the DPPH radical-scavenging activity, where curcumin-Ca chelate had the highest ABTS radical-scavenging activity with a value of 22.79 %.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the TEO-loaded Pickering emulsion stabilized by BCNs/Ag nanoparticles presented uniform spheres with clear boundaries. Pickering emulsions containing curcumin displayed larger, more aggregated droplets of TEO as compared to the emulsions without curcumin, which was related to that the limited interaction between highly crystalline curcumin and stable BCNs/Ag nanoparticles could lead to poor dispersion and increased potential for phase separation [5]. The Pickering emulsions containing curcumin-metal chelates were smaller and more evenly distributed than the droplets containing originally curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin, which was due to the lower crystallinity of curcumin after chelating with the metal, thus improving the stability of Pickering emulsions [6,7]. The particle size distribution (Fig. 2B) further confirmed these observations. The average particle size of Cur-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions was significantly smaller than original curcumin (27.12 ± 0.02 μm) and mechanically treated-curcumin (26.64 ± 0.76 μm). Interestingly, among Cur-metal-loaded Pickering emulsions, Cur-Ca-loaded Pickering emulsion had the smallest average particle size with the value of 18.33 ± 0.69 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy of the prepared active-intelligent emulsion films was analyzed in the present work. As shown in Fig. 3A, all films showed the wide adsorption band at 3000–3650 cm−1, associating with the free stretching vibration of intramolecular and intermolecular hydroxyl groups [36]. The peak at 810 cm−1 was the characteristic peak of carvacrol in TEO, in consistent with the result reported in literature [37]. Additionally, the absorption peaks at 2850–2980 cm−1 was assigned to the tensile vibration of CH [22,38]. For the films loaded with the different structure of curcumin, the peaks appeared at 1206 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and the tensile vibration of C-O-C [39]. While for the films loaded with the curcumin-metal chelates, the OH had shifted, which was due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color characteristics and transmittance of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para:
The color characteristics of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films were described by color characteristics in Table 1 . Type I film showed the highest L
⁎ value (84.75), suggesting that the Type I film were the clearest and the most transparent among these six types films. While the differences of L
⁎ , a
⁎ and b
⁎ values between Type II and III films were related to the destruction of curcumin during the mechanical treatment process. For Type IV, V and VI films, the color characteristics differences were ascribed to that chelation of curcumin with different ions altered the electronic structure and molecular orbital energy levels of curcumin differently, resulting in varying changes in its light absorption properties [7].

Light transmittance property plays an important role in food packaging [22], thus the light transmittance of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films was measured. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the transmittance of Type I film was the highest with a value of 55.56 %, while the transmittance of active-intelligent emulsion films decreased with the addition of the different structure of curcumin. The result was related to that curcumin was incorporated into the KGM film matrix, which created a heterogeneous system. The refractive index (RI) of dispersible phases (the different structure of curcumin) were different from that of the continuous phase (KGM film matrix), resulting in increased light scattering within the film. As light passes through these heterogeneous phases, the differing refractive indexes caused more scattering, thus reducing the overall transmittance [41]. Interestingly, the transmittance of Type VI film was the lowest with a value of 31.34 %, which was due to that the color of formed curcumin-Zn chelate was the darkest, in agreement with the result in Table 1. The result suggested that KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with the curcumin with different structures had potential prevention packaged food from nutrient losses, off-flavors and lipid oxidation caused by light effectively [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3C and D showed the thermal stability of the prepared films from room temperature to 600 °C, where thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) were investigated. TGA analysis revealed that all KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films presented three weight loss stages (Fig. 3C). At the first stage, the weight loss stage occurred at 30–110 °C, which was likely related to the volatilization of free or bound water and TEO in the films [3]. At the second stage, the weight loss appeared at 160–280 °C, which was due to the loss of the plasticizer glycerin, in agreement with our previous results [43]. At the third stage, the weight loss appeared at 290–380 °C, which was due to the thermal decomposition of KGM and BCNs/Ag nanoparticles and the different structure of curcumin, in consistent with the result reported in literature [44]. DTG was the first order differential of TGA. As displayed in Fig. 3D, where the maximum weight loss temperature of Type I film was 334 °C. While the maximum weight loss temperature increased with the addition of curcumin with different structures, which was attributed to that the hydrophobic interaction between KGM and curcumin, resulting in the molecular chains were more tightly and the structure was more stable [23]. In addition, the maximum weight loss temperature of Type IV, V and VI films was higher than that of Type III film (349 °C), which was ascribed to that curcumin-metal chelates had bigger amorphous region than mechanically treated-curcumin, thus the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix was stronger and the structure of films was more compact and stable [45]. Interestingly, Type IV film had the highest maximum weight loss temperature (380 °C), which was due to that the amorphous region of curcumin-Ca chelate was the biggest, thus the interfacial interactions were the strongest, in agreement with the result described in Fig. 1C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical property of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanical property is an important index for food packaging [46], thus the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) were investigated in the present study. As shown in Fig. 3E, the TS value of Type I film was the lowest with a value of 48.23 MPa, while the TS value increased with the addition of curcumin with different structures, which was attributed to the hydrophobic interactions of molecular chains, thus the structure was more compact as compared with that of Type I film [23]. In addition, the TS value of Type IV, V and VI films was higher than that of Type III film, which was related to that curcumin-metal chelates had more amorphous region as compared with mechanically treated-curcumin, thus the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix was stronger and the structure of the films loaded with curcumin-metal chelates was more compact [45]. Interestingly, the TS value of Type IV film was the highest with a value of 68.34 MPa, followed by that of Type V film (62.35 MPa) and Type VI film (56.67 MPa). The TS value differences of curcumin-metal films were due to that the steric hindrances formed by curcumin chelated with different metal ions were different, resulting in the different interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates with the film matrix [30]. For the EAB (Fig. 3F), the EAB value of Type I film was the highest with a value of 20.86 %, while the EAB value decreased with the addition of curcumin with different structures, which was related to the hydrophobic interactions of the different structure of curcumin and the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix that decreased the fluidity of molecular chains [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructures of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructures of films were shown in Fig. 4A, where Type I film showed the most compact, smooth and homogenous surface, in agreement with our previous work [47]. While the surface of Type II and III films became rougher, which was probably due to the presence of curcumin crystals precipitation during the process of the film preparation, in consistent with the results reported in literature [39]. For the Type IV, V and VI films, the surface of films became smoother as compared with that of Type III film, which was related to that the chelation increased the amorphous region of curcumin, thus improving the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and the film matrix and leading to better disperse of curcumin-metal chelates in the matrix. Especially, the surface of Type IV film was the smoothest, which was related to that chelating with Ca2+ would significantly change the crystal structure of curcumin, resulting in the largest amorphous region of curcumin-Ca chelate [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: Contact angle (CA) is a key indicator for the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of films [26], thus the contact angle values of the films were determined in the present work. As shown in Fig. 4B, the CA value increased as a result of the addition of the hydrophobic curcumin with different structures. In addition, the CA value of Type II film (101.32°) was higher than Type III film (98.17°), which was related to the loss of curcumin during mechanical treatment process. For Type IV, V and VI films, the hydrophobicity decreased as compared with that of Type III film, which was attributed to that metal ions introduced additional polar groups, which changed the electron distribution of curcumin molecules, and the chelation formed more stable hydrate structures in water, thus enhancing the dispersion and solubility of curcumin in aqueous solutions by forming hydrogen bonds with water molecules and Van der Waals' force. In addition, the curcumin molecule itself had a strong hydrophobicity, which was easy to form aggregates in water, resulting in low solubility. The formation of chelates could disrupt the formation of these aggregates, allowing curcumin molecules to be more evenly dispersed in the aqueous solution, thus increasing its solubility [48]. Furthermore, the CA value of Type IV film was the smallest with a value of 92.56°, followed by that of Type V film (94.89°) and Type VI film (96.23°). The CA value difference of curcumin-metal films was related to the difference of the outermost electron layer of metal ions [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total phenol content and antioxidant activity of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of total phenolic content in the active-intelligent films is significant for evaluating their antioxidant properties, which play a crucial role in enhancing the fresh-keeping and freshness detection properties of the films for meat products [23], thus the total phenolic content was investigated in the present work. As shown in Fig. 5A, the total phenol content of Type I film was the lowest with a value of 13.36 mg gallic acid/g film, while the total phenol content of the films loaded with the different structure of curcumin increased, which was attributed to that curcumin containing two phenolic hydroxyl had antioxidant activity [7]. Moreover, the total phenol content of Type II film (16.63 mg gallic acid/g film) was higher than that of Type III film (15.78 mg gallic acid/g film), which was related to that the mechanical treatment process caused the loss of curcumin. For Type IV, V and VI films, the total phenol content was significantly increased as compared with that of Type III film, suggesting that chelating with metal ions could improve the stability of curcumin, thereby reducing the destruction of curcumin during preparation and storage, in consistent with the result of thermal stability described in Fig. 3C and D. Especially, Type IV film displayed the highest total phenol content with a value of 17.30 mg gallic acid/g film, suggesting that curcumin-Ca showed the highest stability, in agreement with the result described in Fig. 1E and F.

As presented in Fig. 5B, the trend of the DPPH radical-scavenging activity was similar to that of the total phenol content, showing a positive correlation between DPPH radical-scavenging activity and total phenol content, which was due to that the DPPH radical-scavenging activity was primarily attributed to the antioxidant activity of phenolic hydroxyl groups in curcumin and TEO [49]. The DPPH assay was worked by measuring the ability of antioxidants to donate electrons or hydrogen atoms to neutralize DPPH free radicals, which was indicated by a decrease in absorbance in the present work [49]. For Type IV, V and VI films, the DPPH radical-scavenging activity was significantly improved, which was ascribed to that curcumin-metal chelates had higher content of bioactive ingredients as a result of the higher stability of curcumin-metal chelates [34]. In addition, after chelating with metal ions, the antioxidant ability of metal ions and the curcumin showed synergistic effects to some extent [7]. This result was due to that chelation could redistribute the π electron cloud density in the molecules, changing the electronic structure of curcumin molecules, which would provide more electrons to neutralize free radicals, thus improving the oxidation resistance [7]. The result of ABTS radical-scavenging activity exhibited a similar trend to that of the DPPH radical-scavenging activity. Interestingly, Type IV film had the best antioxidant activity with the DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 69.24 % and 58.66 %, respectively, in agreement with the antioxidant activity of curcumin-metal chelates result described in Fig. 1G and H. The results showed that Type IV film had potentially efficient prevention packaged food from oxidation, thus displaying great potential application in prolonging the shelf life of packaged food [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, water content (WC) and water solubility (WS) of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 2 , the thickness of the Type I film was 0.046 mm, while the thickness of the films increased with the addition of curcumin with different structures, which was due to the increased solid content. Moreover, the thickness of Type IV, V and VI films was thinner than that of Type II and III films, which was ascribed to that curcumin-metal chelates had more amorphous region than original curcumin and mechanically treated-curcumin, thus the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix was stronger, leading to the more compacter structure of films [32].

As described in Table 2, Type I film had the highest WC value (9.93 %), while the WC value decreased as a result of the addition of hydrophobic curcumin with different structures. The WC value of Type II film was higher than that of Type III film, which was related to the destruction of hydrophobic curcumin during the mechanical treatment process. Moreover, the WC value of Type IV, V and VI films was higher than that of Type III film, which was attributed to that the crystallinity of curcumin-metal chelates was smaller than that of curcumin (Fig. 1D), thus easier to bind water. For Type IV, V and VI films, the WC value of Type IV film was the highest with a value of 9.19 %, followed by that of Type V film (8.51 %) and Type VI film (8.25 %), which was due to the amorphous region of Type IV film was the biggest [16]. For the water solubility (WS), the trend was similar to that of water content, where for the films loaded with curcumin-metal chelates, Type IV film had the largest WS with a value of 28.46 %, which was related to that the hydrophobic of Type IV film was the smallest, thus Type IV film could the best dissolved in water, in agreement with the result in Fig. 4B [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and relative oxygen transmission rate (ROT) of KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: The water vapor permeability (WVP) and relative oxygen transmission rate (ROT) of the films are important indicators to evaluate food packaging [47], thus the WVP and ROT of films were investigated in the present study. As displayed in Table 2, the WVP value of Type I film (3.98 × 10−11 g m/Pa s m2) was the highest. While the WVP value was decreased as a result of the loading of curcumin. Especially, the loading of curcumin-metal chelates significantly reduced the WVP value of the KGM-based films. Interestingly, Type IV film displayed the highest WC value(2.87 × 10−11 g m/Pa s m2), followed by Type V film (2.72 × 10−11 g m/Pa s m2) and Type VI film (2.69 × 10−11 g m/Pa s m2), which was due to that the hydrophobic of Type IV film was the weakest, thus the water vapor permeability increased [45].

As shown in Table 2, the relative oxygen transmission rate (ROT) value of the Type I film was the highest (3.12 × 10−3 g/m2 s). For the films loaded with curcumin-metal chelates, the ROT value of Type IV film was the lowest with a value of 1.16 × 10−3 g/m2 s, followed by that of Type V film (1.49 × 10−3 g/m2 s) and Type VI film (1.81 × 10−3 g/m2 s), which was due to that curcumin-Ca chelates had the smallest crystallinity, thus the interfacial interactions between curcumin-Ca chelates and film matrix was the strongest and the structure of films was the most compact, resulting in lengthening the migration path of oxygen molecules in the films [52]. The results suggested that Type IV film could decrease the water vapor permeability and relative oxygen transmission rate, thereby mitigating the food oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of the KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films was investigated using S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 as test microorganisms. As shown in Fig. 6A, the inhibition zone size of films loaded with different structure of curcumin was bigger than that of Type I film, which was due to that curcumin through inhibiting ATP-ase activity, the assembly of protein and destroying cell membrane permeabilization to inhibit the bacteria growth [14]. The inhibition zone size of Type IV, V and VI films was bigger than that of Type II and III films, which was due to that the film-forming solutions loaded with curcumin with different structures were more stable, thus retaining more active ingredients during the preparation process and enhancing the inhibition ability of bacteria. The corresponding diameter of inhibition zone was displayed in Fig. 6B, the inhibition zone diameters of Type IV film against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 were the highest with the values of 1.45 cm and 1.51 cm, respectively, in agreement of the results described in Fig. 6A. Taken together, Type IV film showed the best thermal stability, antioxidant and antibacterial activities. Therefore, Type IV films were used to package the fresh meat for fresh-keeping and freshness detection in the further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fresh-keeping and freshness detection properties of the KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films
Storage quality of the fresh pork
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the fresh-keeping properties of different packaging materials, the quality changes in packaged fresh pork were assessed by measurements of TVB-N, pH, hardness, and springiness. As shown in Fig. 7A and B, the TVB-N and pH values exhibited an increasing trend with the extension of storage time. This increase could be attributed to the gradual decomposition of proteins in the fresh pork by microorganisms, leading to the production of basic nitrogen-containing toxic substances such as ammonia and amines. Since then, these decomposition substances would combine with organic acids to generate the volatile basic nitrogen compounds during the meat spoilage process, thereby elevating the TVB-N and pH values [28]. At the beginning of the storage time, the TVB-N value of the pork was 7.12 mg/100 g, which was significantly lower than the critical threshold defined by the national standard GB 2707–2016 for fresh meat (15.00 mg/100 g), confirming the freshness of the pork. However, after 4 days of storage, the TVB-N values of pork without packaging and pork packaged with cling film rose to 27.65 mg/100 g and 27.00 mg/100 g, respectively, which were both markedly higher than the critical value of spoiled meat (25.00 mg/100 g) [23], indicating complete spoilage of the pork. In contrast, pork packaged with pure KGM film and Type IV film showed TVB-N values of 24.64 mg/100 g and 23.93 mg/100 g, respectively, which were both lower than the spoilage threshold, suggesting that the pork was still in a moderately fresh state. Regarding pH values, after 4 days of storage, pork without packaging and packaged with cling film exhibited pH values of 6.76 and 6.73, respectively, both exceeding the spoilage threshold (pH > 6.7) and indicating spoilage. Conversely, the pH values of pork packaged with pure KGM film and Type IV film were 6.65 and 6.62, respectively, which were both lower than the critical threshold, indicating a better preservation state. Interestingly, the pork packaged with Type IV film consistently showed the lowest TVB-N and pH values as compared with all the other pork, suggesting that Type IV film displayed the best effects on the inhibition of microbial growth, thereby prolonging the shelf life of the pork [53]. In addition to chemical indicators, the textural properties of the pork packaged, including hardness and springiness, also declined with the increase of storage time (Fig. 6C and D). This decline is likely due to the hydrolysis of muscle proteins and the breakdown of the meat structure, leading to a deterioration in texture [28]. However, after 5 days of storage, pork packaged with Type IV film exhibited the highest hardness (47.96 N), followed by pork packaged with pure KGM film (45.19 N), pork packaged with cling film (40.98 N), and pork without packaging (38.99 N). A similar trend was observed in the springiness of the pork, where the springiness showed the highest value (0.39 %) in the group of the pork packaged with Type IV film. These results clearly demonstrated that Type IV film provided superior fresh-keeping properties, which could be attributed to its excellent barrier properties and antibacterial activity. Consequently, the use of Type IV film represents a promising approach for improving the shelf life and quality of fresh pork in commercial applications.

Nitrogenous compounds produced by enzymatic and microbial degradation of food will increase the pH of the microenvironment inside food packaging. Therefore, active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with natural pigments that changes in color at different pH levels can be used to detect meat freshness [28]. In the present work, the prepared active-intelligent emulsion films (Type IV film) were used for the freshness detection of fresh pork in the present work. The color change of Type IV film at different pH value was displayed in Fig. 7E. As illustrated in Fig. 7F, the color parameters of Type IV film were changed as storage time increased, where the a
⁎ value increased from 2.59 to 18.17, the b
⁎ value decreased from 39.39 to 20.67, and ΔE⁎ values increased from 1.58 to 12.02. Furthermore, the color of Type IV film shifted from yellow to red (Fig. 7E), which was related to that the accumulation of nitrogenous substances lead to the increase of pH value in the packaging microenvironment. The results showed that active-intelligent emulsion films could effectively detect the freshness of pork [54–56].

The aim of this study was to develop KGM-based active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with different curcumin-metal chelates, where six types of films were prepared and their corresponding characterizations were compared. Firstly, three types of curcumin-metal chelates including curcumin-Ca, curcumin-Mg and curcumin-Zn chelates were prepared and their corresponding characterization was investigated in this study. UV–vis, FTIR and XRD results showed that curcumin chelated with metal ions successfully. Moreover, curcumin-Ca chelate had the best thermal stability and antioxidant activity. Then, the prepared curcumin chelates were incorporated into the KGM-based films through Pickering emulsion approach, where the effects of the different curcumin chelates on the properties of KGM-based films were investigated in this study. The FTIR and XRD results showed that curcumin-metal chelates combined with the film matrix successfully. Meanwhile, the microstructure and contact angle results showed that chelation with metal ions improved the interfacial interactions between curcumin-metal chelates and film matrix. Interestingly, the Type IV film loaded with curcumin-Ca chelates displayed the best thermal stability with the highest temperature of maximum weight loss at 380 °C, the highest total phenol content (17.31 mg gallic acid/g film), the best antioxidant activity with DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging activity values of 69.24 % and 58.66 %, respectively, and the best antibacterial activity. Since then, Type IV film was used for the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of pork. The results showed that the quality of pork packaged with Type IV film was the best, and the color of the Type IV film gradually changed from yellow to red, indicating that the active-intelligent emulsion films did not only show excellent preservation properties for fresh pork, but also display good pH colorimetric response performance. Therefore, the prepared active-intelligent emulsion films loaded with curcumin-Ca chelates have great potential application in the fresh-keeping and freshness detection of fresh meat.
